revision.text:
[[File:Higurashimon.JPG|thumb|right|The south gate of Yusuhara Shrine is called ''Higurashimon'', meaning a "gate from sunrise till sunset"]] {{nihongo|'''Yusuhara Hachiman-gū'''|柞原八幡宮||}}, also known as '''Yasuhara Shrine''', is a [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Oita, Oita]] on the island of [[Kyushu]].<ref name="kotodamayama">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/yasuhara-hachiman/ "Yasuhara Hachimangu"]; retrieved 2012-10-25.</ref>
==History== Yusuhara is believed to have been built in the early 9th century. It was established as a branch shrine temple (''miyadera'') of ''[[Usa jingū]].''<ref>Oita City Tourist Association, [http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/english/hannichi.html "Yusuhara Hachiman Shrine"]; Haruko Nawata, [http://books.google.com/books?id=saX0gHc91fkC&pg=PA124&lpg=PA124&dq=yusuhara+hachimangu&source=bl&ots=GS5ILq1ipI&sig=f6dYQXTFh_0sm_nV6zMlnz-6W2E&hl=en&sa=X&ei=8paKUKasMub50gGdkoDYDA&ved=0CDkQ6AEwBjgK#v=onepage&q=yusuhara%20hachimangu&f=false ''Women Religious Leaders in Japan's Christian Century, 1549-1650,'' p. 124n50]; retrieved 2012-10-25.</ref>
Yusuhara was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of the old [[Bungo Province]]. It serves today as one of the ''ichinomiya'' of [[Niigata Prefecture]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3]; retrieved 2012-10-25.</ref> The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' are: * {{nihongo|Chuai tenno|仲哀天皇||}}<ref name="kotodamayama"/> * {{nihongo|Emperor Oojin|応神天皇||}}<ref name="kotodamayama"/> * {{nihongo|Empress Jingu|神功皇后||}}<ref name="kotodamayama"/>
In 1916, the rank of the shrine was changed from ''kensha'' to ''Kokuhei Shō-sha''.<ref>Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [http://books.google.com/books?id=6y8VAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA270&lpg=PA270&dq= ''The political philosophy of modern Shintō: a study of the state religion of Japan,'' p. 270.</ref>
==Related pages== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links == {{Commons category-inline|Yusuhara Hachimangu}} * [http://www.digital.archives.go.jp/gallery/view/category/categoryArchivesEn/0200000000/0204000000 Map of Yasuhara Hachimangu (''Bungo-no-Kuni Ohno-gun Yusuhara Hachimangu zu'') at National Archives of Japan]
{{Shinto shrine}}
<!-- {{coord|_|_|_|N|136|_|_|E|source:___|display=title}} -->
{{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-struct-stub}}
[[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]]
[[ja:柞原八幡宮]]

==History== Yusuhara is believed to have been built in the early 9th century. It was established as a branch shrine temple (''miyadera'') of ''[[Usa jingū]].''<ref>Oita City Tourist Association, [http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/english/hannichi.html "Yusuhara Hachiman Shrine"]; Haruko Nawata, [http://books.google.com/books?id=saX0gHc91fkC&pg=PA124&lpg=PA124&dq=yusuhara+hachimangu&source=bl&ots=GS5ILq1ipI&sig=f6dYQXTFh_0sm_nV6zMlnz-6W2E&hl=en&sa=X&ei=8paKUKasMub50gGdkoDYDA&ved=0CDkQ6AEwBjgK#v=onepage&q=yusuhara%20hachimangu&f=false ''Women Religious Leaders in Japan's Christian Century, 1549-1650,'' p. 124n50]; retrieved 2012-10-25.</ref>
Yusuhara was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of the old [[Bungo Province]]. It serves today as one of the ''ichinomiya'' of [[Niigata Prefecture]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3]; retrieved 2012-10-25.</ref> The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' are: * {{nihongo|Chuai tenno|仲哀天皇||}}<ref name="kotodamayama"/> * {{nihongo|Emperor Oojin|応神天皇||}}<ref name="kotodamayama"/> * {{nihongo|Empress Jingu|神功皇后||}}<ref name="kotodamayama"/>
In 1916, the shrine was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}} .<ref>Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [http://books.google.com/books?id=6y8VAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA270&lpg=PA270&dq= ''The political philosophy of modern Shintō: a study of the state religion of Japan,'' p. 270.</ref>
==Related pages== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links == {{Commons category-inline|Yusuhara Hachimangu}} * [http://www.digital.archives.go.jp/gallery/view/category/categoryArchivesEn/0200000000/0204000000 Map of Yasuhara Hachimangu (''Bungo-no-Kuni Ohno-gun Yusuhara Hachimangu zu'') at National Archives of Japan]

In 1916, the shrine was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}} .<ref>Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [http://books.google.com/books?id=6y8VAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA270&lpg=PA270&dq= ''The political philosophy of modern Shintō: a study of the state religion of Japan,'' p. 270].</ref>

It was established as a branch shrine temple (''miyadera'') of ''[[Usa jingū]].''<ref>Oita City Tourist Association, [http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/english/hannichi.html "Yusuhara Hachiman Shrine"] {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080611194605/http://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/english/hannichi.html |date=June 11, 2008 }}; Haruko Nawata, [https://books.google.com/books?id=saX0gHc91fkC&pg=PA124&lpg=PA124&dq=yusuhara+hachimangu&source=bl&ots=GS5ILq1ipI&sig=f6dYQXTFh_0sm_nV6zMlnz-6W2E&hl=en&sa=X&ei=8paKUKasMub50gGdkoDYDA&ved=0CDkQ6AEwBjgK#v=onepage&q=yusuhara%20hachimangu&f=false ''Women Religious Leaders in Japan's Christian Century, 1549-1650,'' p. 124n50]; retrieved 2012-10-25.</ref>
Yusuhara was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of the old [[Bungo Province]]. It serves today as one of the ''ichinomiya'' of [[Niigata Prefecture]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3]; retrieved 2012-10-25.</ref> The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' are: * {{nihongo|Chuai tenno|仲哀天皇||}}<ref name="kotodamayama"/> * {{nihongo|Emperor Oojin|応神天皇||}}<ref name="kotodamayama"/> * {{nihongo|Empress Jingu|神功皇后||}}<ref name="kotodamayama"/>
In 1916, the shrine was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}} .<ref>Holton, Daniel Clarence. (1922). [https://books.google.com/books?id=6y8VAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA270&lpg=PA270&dq= ''The political philosophy of modern Shintō: a study of the state religion of Japan,'' p. 270].</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōita, Ōita, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yusuhara Hachiman-gū
柞原八幡宮 | image = YusuharaHachimangu Shaden.JPG | caption = ''Shaden'' of Yusuhara Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|14|18.2|N|131|33|3.6|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = c.830 | date_destroyed = | location = 987 Yawata, Ōita-shi, Ōita-ken | website = {{Official|1=https://oita-yusuhara.com}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = March 15 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yusuhara Hachiman-gū'''|柞原八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōita, Ōita | Ōita]], [[Ōita Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bungo Province]], the other being the [[Sasamuta Shrine|Sasamuta Jinja]], also in Ōita.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is also cometimes refrred to as {{nihongo|'''Yusubara Hachiman-gū'''|由原八幡宮||}}. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''[[Hachiman]]'''|八幡||}}, here identified as the trinity of [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Empress Jingū]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend, which dates from the [[Kamakura period]] states that in the year 827, a monk of [[Enryaku-ji]] named Konki worshipped at [[Usa Hachiman-gū]] for 1000 days and received an oracle which stated that the ''kami'' Hachiman would bless Bungo Province. A mysterious white banner flew over Kakugo, [[Ōita District, Ōita| Ōita]] and was presented to [[Emperor Ninmyo]], who ordered the governor of Bungo Province to construct this shrine. From the year 998 onwards, it became the practice to reconstruct the shrine buildings at 33 year intervals, the same as at Usa Hachiman-gū. Despite its dispute with Sasumuta Jinja, which as an equally ancient claim to the title, it has been referred to as the "Bungo ''ichinomiya'' since the 12th century, because of the shrine's proximity to the ''[[kokufu]]'' of Bungo Province. The shrine was supported by the [[Ōtomo clan]] from the time they entered the province as ''[[shugo]]'' during the Kamakura period. In the [[Sengoku period]], it was destroyed by [[Ōtomo Sōrin]] after his conversion to Christianity, but was rebuilt in the early [[Edo period]] and supported by the successive ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Funai Domain]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. In 1912, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} .{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Yusuhara hachimangu Big gate.JPG|Torii Gate Higurashimon.JPG|Nandaimon Yusuhara hachimangu Nishimon.JPG|Nishimon Yusuhara hachimangu Roumon.JPG|'Moshidono, Heiden, Rōmon Inside Roumon.JPG|Inside the shrine buildings ==Cultural properties== ===Natural Monuments=== * ''[[Camphora officinarum]]'', National Natural Monument.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2851|title=柞原八幡宮のクス|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} Claimed to be over 3000 years old. Mentioned by [[Ino Tadataka]] in his 1811 survey of Japan ===[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]=== *'''Yusuhara Hachiman-gu''' (10 structures), Edo Period.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00004531|title=柞原八幡宮|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} ** Honden, 1850 ** Mōshidono, 1752 ** Haiden, 1759 ** Rōmon, 1760 ** Tōhōden, 1757 ** Saihōden, 1757 ** East Corridor, 1798 ** West Corridor, 1798 ** West Gate, late Edo Period ** South Great Gate, 1870, with storehouse including 4 documents: blueprints for Main Shrine and Rōmon, 2 maps of precincts) * '''[[Tachi]] sword''', Kamakura period, inscribed "Kunimune"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6405|title=太刀〈銘国宗／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Tachi sword''', Kamakura period, inscribed "Minamoto no Kuni?"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6404|title=太刀〈銘源国（以下一字竝年号不明）／〉〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''[[Halberd]]''', Nanboku-chō period, inscribed "Kunishige" and "Hachiman Daibodhisattva Tenma Daijizai Tenjin"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6406|title=薙刀直シ刀〈銘国重八幡大菩薩天満大自在天神／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Bronze Buddha statue''', {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōita, Ōita, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yusuhara Hachiman-gū
柞原八幡宮 | image = YusuharaHachimangu Shaden.JPG | caption = ''Shaden'' of Yusuhara Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|14|18.2|N|131|33|3.6|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = c.830 | date_destroyed = | location = 987 Yawata, Ōita-shi, Ōita-ken | website = {{Official|1=https://oita-yusuhara.com}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = March 15 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yusuhara Hachiman-gū'''|柞原八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōita, Ōita | Ōita]], [[Ōita Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bungo Province]], the other being the [[Sasamuta Shrine|Sasamuta Jinja]], also in Ōita.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is also cometimes refrred to as {{nihongo|'''Yusubara Hachiman-gū'''|由原八幡宮||}}. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''[[Hachiman]]'''|八幡||}}, here identified as the trinity of [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Empress Jingū]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend, which dates from the [[Kamakura period]] states that in the year 827, a monk of [[Enryaku-ji]] named Konki worshipped at [[Usa Hachiman-gū]] for 1000 days and received an oracle which stated that the ''kami'' Hachiman would bless Bungo Province. A mysterious white banner flew over Kakugo, [[Ōita District, Ōita| Ōita]] and was presented to [[Emperor Ninmyo]], who ordered the governor of Bungo Province to construct this shrine. From the year 998 onwards, it became the practice to reconstruct the shrine buildings at 33 year intervals, the same as at Usa Hachiman-gū. Despite its dispute with Sasumuta Jinja, which as an equally ancient claim to the title, it has been referred to as the "Bungo ''ichinomiya'' since the 12th century, because of the shrine's proximity to the ''[[kokufu]]'' of Bungo Province. The shrine was supported by the [[Ōtomo clan]] from the time they entered the province as ''[[shugo]]'' during the Kamakura period. In the [[Sengoku period]], it was destroyed by [[Ōtomo Sōrin]] after his conversion to Christianity, but was rebuilt in the early [[Edo period]] and supported by the successive ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Funai Domain]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. In 1912, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} .{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Yusuhara hachimangu Big gate.JPG|Torii Gate Higurashimon.JPG|Nandaimon Yusuhara hachimangu Nishimon.JPG|Nishimon Yusuhara hachimangu Roumon.JPG|'Moshidono, Heiden, Rōmon Inside Roumon.JPG|Inside the shrine buildings ==Cultural properties== ===Natural Monuments=== * ''[[Camphora officinarum]]'', National Natural Monument.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2851|title=柞原八幡宮のクス|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} Claimed to be over 3000 years old. Mentioned by [[Ino Tadataka]] in his 1811 survey of Japan ===[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]=== *'''Yusuhara Hachiman-gu''' (10 structures), Edo Period.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00004531|title=柞原八幡宮|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} ** Honden, 1850 ** Mōshidono, 1752 ** Haiden, 1759 ** Rōmon, 1760 ** Tōhōden, 1757 ** Saihōden, 1757 ** East Corridor, 1798 ** West Corridor, 1798 ** West Gate, late Edo Period ** South Great Gate, 1870, with storehouse including 4 documents: blueprints for Main Shrine and Rōmon, 2 maps of precincts) * '''[[Tachi]] sword''', Kamakura period, inscribed "Kunimune"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6405|title=太刀〈銘国宗／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Tachi sword''', Kamakura period, inscribed "Minamoto no Kuni?"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6404|title=太刀〈銘源国（以下一字竝年号不明）／〉〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''[[Halberd]]''', Nanboku-chō period, inscribed "Kunishige" and "Hachiman Daibodhisattva Tenma Daijizai Tenjin"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6406|title=薙刀直シ刀〈銘国重八幡大菩薩天満大自在天神／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Bronze Buddha statue''', [[Asuka period]]. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4648 |title=銅造仏像（社伝阿弥陀如來立像）|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Armor''', complete set, Muromachi period, {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/7221 |title=白檀塗浅葱糸威腹巻〈兜・大袖・小具足付／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} * '''Yusuhara Hachiman-gu old documents''', Heian period, 17 scrolls with 216 documents. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/10379 |title=柞原八幡宮文書（二百十六通）|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Yusuhara-hachimangu}} *[https://oita-yusuhara.com Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Bungo |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6406|title=薙刀直シ刀〈銘国重八幡大菩薩天満大自在天神／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Bronze Buddha statue''', [[Asuka period]]. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4648 |title=銅造仏像（社伝阿弥陀如來立像）|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Armor''', complete set, Muromachi period, {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/7221 |title=白檀塗浅葱糸威腹巻〈兜・大袖・小具足付／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} * '''Yusuhara Hachiman-gu old documents''', Heian period, 17 scrolls with 216 documents. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/10379 |title=柞原八幡宮文書（二百十六通）|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōita, Ōita, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yusuhara Hachiman-gū
柞原八幡宮 | image = YusuharaHachimangu Shaden.JPG | caption = ''Shaden'' of Yusuhara Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|14|18.2|N|131|33|3.6|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = c.830 | date_destroyed = | location = 987 Yawata, Ōita-shi, Ōita-ken | website = {{Official|1=https://oita-yusuhara.com}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = March 15 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yusuhara Hachiman-gū'''|柞原八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōita, Ōita | Ōita]], [[Ōita Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bungo Province]], the other being the [[Sasamuta Shrine|Sasamuta Jinja]], also in Ōita.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is also sometimes referred to as {{nihongo|'''Yusubara Hachiman-gū'''|由原八幡宮||}}. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''[[Hachiman]]'''|八幡||}}, here identified as the trinity of [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Empress Jingū]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend, which dates from the [[Kamakura period]] states that in the year 827, a monk of [[Enryaku-ji]] named Konki worshipped at [[Usa Hachiman-gū]] for 1000 days and received an oracle which stated that the ''kami'' Hachiman would bless Bungo Province. A mysterious white banner flew over Kakugo, [[Ōita District, Ōita| Ōita]] and was presented to [[Emperor Ninmyo]], who ordered the governor of Bungo Province to construct this shrine. From the year 998 onwards, it became the practice to reconstruct the shrine buildings at 33 year intervals, the same as at Usa Hachiman-gū. Despite its dispute with Sasumuta Jinja, which as an equally ancient claim to the title, it has been referred to as the "Bungo ''ichinomiya'' since the 12th century, because of the shrine's proximity to the ''[[kokufu]]'' of Bungo Province. The shrine was supported by the [[Ōtomo clan]] from the time they entered the province as ''[[shugo]]'' during the Kamakura period. In the [[Sengoku period]], it was destroyed by [[Ōtomo Sōrin]] after his conversion to Christianity, but was rebuilt in the early [[Edo period]] and supported by the successive ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Funai Domain]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. In 1912, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} .{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Yusuhara hachimangu Big gate.JPG|Torii Gate Higurashimon.JPG|Nandaimon Yusuhara hachimangu Nishimon.JPG|Nishimon Yusuhara hachimangu Roumon.JPG|'Moshidono, Heiden, Rōmon Inside Roumon.JPG|Inside the shrine buildings ==Cultural properties== ===Natural Monuments=== * ''[[Camphora officinarum]]'', National Natural Monument.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2851|title=柞原八幡宮のクス|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} Claimed to be over 3000 years old. Mentioned by [[Ino Tadataka]] in his 1811 survey of Japan ===[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]=== *'''Yusuhara Hachiman-gu''' (10 structures), Edo Period.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00004531|title=柞原八幡宮|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} ** Honden, 1850 ** Mōshidono, 1752 ** Haiden, 1759 ** Rōmon, 1760 ** Tōhōden, 1757 ** Saihōden, 1757 ** East Corridor, 1798 ** West Corridor, 1798 ** West Gate, late Edo Period ** South Great Gate, 1870, with storehouse including 4 documents: blueprints for Main Shrine and Rōmon, 2 maps of precincts) * '''[[Tachi]] sword''', Kamakura period, inscribed "Kunimune"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6405|title=太刀〈銘国宗／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Tachi sword''', Kamakura period, inscribed "Minamoto no Kuni?"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6404|title=太刀〈銘源国（以下一字竝年号不明）／〉〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''[[Halberd]]''', Nanboku-chō period, inscribed "Kunishige" and "Hachiman Daibodhisattva Tenma Daijizai Tenjin"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6406|title=薙刀直シ刀〈銘国重八幡大菩薩天満大自在天神／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Bronze Buddha statue''', [[Asuka period]]. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4648 |title=銅造仏像（社伝阿弥陀如來立像）|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Armor''', complete set, Muromachi period, {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/7221 |title=白檀塗浅葱糸威腹巻〈兜・大袖・小具足付／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} * '''Yusuhara Hachiman-gu old documents''', Heian period, 17 scrolls with 216 documents. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/10379 |title=柞原八幡宮文書（二百十六通）|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Yusuhara-hachimangu}} *[https://oita-yusuhara.com Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Bungo Province]] [[Category:Ōita (city)]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]][[en:Yusuhara Hachimangū]][[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōita, Ōita, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yusuhara Hachiman-gū
柞原八幡宮 | image = YusuharaHachimangu Shaden.JPG | caption = ''Shaden'' of Yusuhara Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|14|18.2|N|131|33|3.6|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = c.830 | date_destroyed = | location = 987 Yawata, Ōita-shi, Ōita-ken | website = {{Official|1=https://oita-yusuhara.com}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = March 15 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yusuhara Hachiman-gū'''|柞原八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōita, Ōita | Ōita]], [[Ōita Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bungo Province]], the other being the [[Sasamuta Shrine|Sasamuta Jinja]], also in Ōita.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is also sometimes referred to as {{nihongo|'''Yusubara Hachiman-gū'''|由原八幡宮||}}. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''[[Hachiman]]'''|八幡||}}, here identified as the trinity of [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Empress Jingū]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend, which dates from the [[Kamakura period]] states that in the year 827, a monk of [[Enryaku-ji]] named Konki worshipped at [[Usa Hachiman-gū]] for 1000 days and received an oracle which stated that the ''kami'' Hachiman would bless Bungo Province. A mysterious white banner flew over Kakugo, [[Ōita District, Ōita| Ōita]] and was presented to [[Emperor Ninmyo]], who ordered the governor of Bungo Province to construct this shrine. From the year 998 onwards, it became the practice to reconstruct the shrine buildings at 33 year intervals, the same as at Usa Hachiman-gū. Despite its dispute with Sasumuta Jinja, which as an equally ancient claim to the title, it has been referred to as the "Bungo ''ichinomiya'' since the 12th century, because of the shrine's proximity to the ''[[kokufu]]'' of Bungo Province. The shrine was supported by the [[Ōtomo clan]] from the time they entered the province as ''[[shugo]]'' during the Kamakura period. In the [[Sengoku period]], it was destroyed by [[Ōtomo Sōrin]] after his conversion to Christianity, but was rebuilt in the early [[Edo period]] and supported by the successive ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Funai Domain]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. In 1912, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} .{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Yusuhara hachimangu Big gate.JPG|Torii Gate Higurashimon.JPG|Nandaimon Yusuhara hachimangu Nishimon.JPG|Nishimon Yusuhara hachimangu Roumon.JPG|'Moshidono, Heiden, Rōmon Inside Roumon.JPG|Inside the shrine buildings ==Cultural properties== ===Natural Monuments=== * ''[[Camphora officinarum]]'', National Natural Monument.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2851|title=柞原八幡宮のクス|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} Claimed to be over 3000 years old. Mentioned by [[Ino Tadataka]] in his 1811 survey of Japan ===[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]=== *'''Yusuhara Hachiman-gu''' (10 structures), Edo Period.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00004531|title=柞原八幡宮|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} ** Honden, 1850 ** Mōshidono, 1752 ** Haiden, 1759 ** Rōmon, 1760 ** Tōhōden, 1757 ** Saihōden, 1757 ** East Corridor, 1798 ** West Corridor, 1798 ** West Gate, late Edo Period ** South Great Gate, 1870, with storehouse including 4 documents: blueprints for Main Shrine and Rōmon, 2 maps of precincts) * '''[[Tachi]] sword''', Kamakura period, inscribed "Kunimune"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6405|title=太刀〈銘国宗／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Tachi sword''', Kamakura period, inscribed "Minamoto no Kuni?"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6404|title=太刀〈銘源国（以下一字竝年号不明）／〉〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''[[Halberd]]''', Nanboku-chō period, inscribed "Kunishige" and "Hachiman Daibodhisattva Tenma Daijizai Tenjin"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6406|title=薙刀直シ刀〈銘国重八幡大菩薩天満大自在天神／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Bronze Buddha statue''', [[Asuka period]]. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4648 |title=銅造仏像（社伝阿弥陀如來立像）|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Armor''', complete set, Muromachi period, {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/7221 |title=白檀塗浅葱糸威腹巻〈兜・大袖・小具足付／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} * '''Yusuhara Hachiman-gu old documents''', Heian period, 17 scrolls with 216 documents. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/10379 |title=柞原八幡宮文書（二百十六通）|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Yusuhara-hachimangu}} *[https://oita-yusuhara.com Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Bungo Province]] [[Category:Ōita (city)]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Yusuhara Hachimangū]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Yusuhara Hachimangū]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōita, Ōita, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yusuhara Hachiman-gū
柞原八幡宮 | image = YusuharaHachimangu Shaden.JPG | caption = ''Shaden'' of Yusuhara Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|14|18.2|N|131|33|3.6|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = c.830 | date_destroyed = | location = 987 Yawata, Ōita-shi, Ōita-ken | website = {{Official|1=https://oita-yusuhara.com}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = March 15 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yusuhara Hachiman-gū'''|柞原八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōita, Ōita | Ōita]], [[Ōita Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bungo Province]], the other being the [[Sasamuta Shrine|Sasamuta Jinja]], also in Ōita.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is also sometimes referred to as {{nihongo|'''Yusubara Hachiman-gū'''|由原八幡宮||}}. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''[[Hachiman]]'''|八幡||}}, here identified as the trinity of [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Empress Jingū]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend, which dates from the [[Kamakura period]] states that in the year 827, a monk of [[Enryaku-ji]] named Konki worshipped at [[Usa Hachiman-gū]] for 1000 days and received an oracle which stated that the ''kami'' Hachiman would bless Bungo Province. A mysterious white banner flew over Kakugo, [[Ōita District, Ōita| Ōita]] and was presented to [[Emperor Ninmyo]], who ordered the governor of Bungo Province to construct this shrine. From the year 998 onwards, it became the practice to reconstruct the shrine buildings at 33 year intervals, the same as at Usa Hachiman-gū. Despite its dispute with Sasumuta Jinja, which as an equally ancient claim to the title, it has been referred to as the "Bungo ''ichinomiya'' since the 12th century, because of the shrine's proximity to the ''[[kokufu]]'' of Bungo Province. The shrine was supported by the [[Ōtomo clan]] from the time they entered the province as ''[[shugo]]'' during the Kamakura period. In the [[Sengoku period]], it was destroyed by [[Ōtomo Sōrin]] after his conversion to Christianity, but was rebuilt in the early [[Edo period]] and supported by the successive ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Funai Domain]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. In 1912, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} .{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Yusuhara hachimangu Big gate.JPG|Torii Gate Higurashimon.JPG|Nandaimon Yusuhara hachimangu Nishimon.JPG|Nishimon Yusuhara hachimangu Roumon.JPG|'Moshidono, Heiden, Rōmon Inside Roumon.JPG|Inside the shrine buildings ==Cultural properties== ===Natural Monuments=== * ''[[Camphora officinarum]]'', National Natural Monument.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2851|title=柞原八幡宮のクス|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} Claimed to be over 3000 years old. Mentioned by [[Ino Tadataka]] in his 1811 survey of Japan ===[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]=== *'''Yusuhara Hachiman-gu''' (10 structures), Edo Period.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00004531|title=柞原八幡宮|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} ** Honden, 1850 ** Mōshidono, 1752 ** Haiden, 1759 ** Rōmon, 1760 ** Tōhōden, 1757 ** Saihōden, 1757 ** East Corridor, 1798 ** West Corridor, 1798 ** West Gate, late Edo Period ** South Great Gate, 1870, with storehouse including 4 documents: blueprints for Main Shrine and Rōmon, 2 maps of precincts) * '''[[Tachi]] sword''', Kamakura period, inscribed "Kunimune"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6405|title=太刀〈銘国宗／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Tachi sword''', Kamakura period, inscribed "Minamoto no Kuni?"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6404|title=太刀〈銘源国（以下一字竝年号不明）／〉〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''[[Halberd]]''', Nanboku-chō period, inscribed "Kunishige" and "Hachiman Daibodhisattva Tenma Daijizai Tenjin"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6406|title=薙刀直シ刀〈銘国重八幡大菩薩天満大自在天神／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Bronze Buddha statue''', [[Asuka period]]. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4648 |title=銅造仏像（社伝阿弥陀如來立像）|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Armor''', complete set, Muromachi period, {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/7221 |title=白檀塗浅葱糸威腹巻〈兜・大袖・小具足付／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} * '''Yusuhara Hachiman-gu old documents''', Heian period, 17 scrolls with 216 documents. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/10379 |title=柞原八幡宮文書（二百十六通）|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Yusuhara-hachimangu}} *[https://oita-yusuhara.com Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Bungo Province]] [[Category:Ōita (city)]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Yusuhara Hachimangū]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[en:Yusuhara Hachimangū]][[en:Yusuhara Hachimangū]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōita, Ōita, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yusuhara Hachiman-gū
柞原八幡宮 | image = YusuharaHachimangu Shaden.JPG | caption = ''Shaden'' of Yusuhara Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|14|18.2|N|131|33|3.6|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = c.830 | date_destroyed = | location = 987 Yawata, Ōita-shi, Ōita-ken | website = {{Official|1=https://oita-yusuhara.com}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = March 15 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yusuhara Hachiman-gū'''|柞原八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōita, Ōita | Ōita]], [[Ōita Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bungo Province]], the other being the [[Sasamuta Shrine|Sasamuta Jinja]], also in Ōita.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is also sometimes referred to as {{nihongo|'''Yusubara Hachiman-gū'''|由原八幡宮||}}. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''[[Hachiman]]'''|八幡||}}, here identified as the trinity of [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Empress Jingū]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend, which dates from the [[Kamakura period]] states that in the year 827, a monk of [[Enryaku-ji]] named Konki worshipped at [[Usa Hachiman-gū]] for 1000 days and received an oracle which stated that the ''kami'' Hachiman would bless Bungo Province. A mysterious white banner flew over Kakugo, [[Ōita District, Ōita| Ōita]] and was presented to [[Emperor Ninmyo]], who ordered the governor of Bungo Province to construct this shrine. From the year 998 onwards, it became the practice to reconstruct the shrine buildings at 33 year intervals, the same as at Usa Hachiman-gū. Despite its dispute with Sasumuta Jinja, which as an equally ancient claim to the title, it has been referred to as the "Bungo ''ichinomiya'' since the 12th century, because of the shrine's proximity to the ''[[kokufu]]'' of Bungo Province. The shrine was supported by the [[Ōtomo clan]] from the time they entered the province as ''[[shugo]]'' during the Kamakura period. In the [[Sengoku period]], it was destroyed by [[Ōtomo Sōrin]] after his conversion to Christianity, but was rebuilt in the early [[Edo period]] and supported by the successive ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Funai Domain]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. In 1912, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} .{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Yusuhara hachimangu Big gate.JPG|Torii Gate Higurashimon.JPG|Nandaimon Yusuhara hachimangu Nishimon.JPG|Nishimon Yusuhara hachimangu Roumon.JPG|'Moshidono, Heiden, Rōmon Inside Roumon.JPG|Inside the shrine buildings ==Cultural properties== ===Natural Monuments=== * ''[[Camphora officinarum]]'', National Natural Monument.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2851|title=柞原八幡宮のクス|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} Claimed to be over 3000 years old. Mentioned by [[Ino Tadataka]] in his 1811 survey of Japan ===[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]=== *'''Yusuhara Hachiman-gu''' (10 structures), Edo Period.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00004531|title=柞原八幡宮|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} ** Honden, 1850 ** Mōshidono, 1752 ** Haiden, 1759 ** Rōmon, 1760 ** Tōhōden, 1757 ** Saihōden, 1757 ** East Corridor, 1798 ** West Corridor, 1798 ** West Gate, late Edo Period ** South Great Gate, 1870, with storehouse including 4 documents: blueprints for Main Shrine and Rōmon, 2 maps of precincts) * '''[[Tachi]] sword''', Kamakura period, inscribed "Kunimune"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6405|title=太刀〈銘国宗／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Tachi sword''', Kamakura period, inscribed "Minamoto no Kuni?"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6404|title=太刀〈銘源国（以下一字竝年号不明）／〉〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''[[Halberd]]''', Nanboku-chō period, inscribed "Kunishige" and "Hachiman Daibodhisattva Tenma Daijizai Tenjin"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6406|title=薙刀直シ刀〈銘国重八幡大菩薩天満大自在天神／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Bronze Buddha statue''', [[Asuka period]]. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4648 |title=銅造仏像（社伝阿弥陀如來立像）|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Armor''', complete set, Muromachi period, {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/7221 |title=白檀塗浅葱糸威腹巻〈兜・大袖・小具足付／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} * '''Yusuhara Hachiman-gu old documents''', Heian period, 17 scrolls with 216 documents. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/10379 |title=柞原八幡宮文書（二百十六通）|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Yusuhara-hachimangu}} *[https://oita-yusuhara.com Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Bungo Province]] [[Category:Ōita (city)]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia25]] [[en:Yusuhara Hachimangū]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia25]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] [[en:Yusuhara Hachimangū]][[en:Yusuhara Hachimangū]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōita, Ōita, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yusuhara Hachiman-gū
柞原八幡宮 | image = YusuharaHachimangu Shaden.JPG | caption = ''Shaden'' of Yusuhara Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|14|18.2|N|131|33|3.6|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = c.830 | date_destroyed = | location = 987 Yawata, Ōita-shi, Ōita-ken | website = {{Official|1=https://oita-yusuhara.com}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = March 15 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yusuhara Hachiman-gū'''|柞原八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōita, Ōita | Ōita]], [[Ōita Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bungo Province]], the other being the [[Sasamuta Shrine|Sasamuta Jinja]], also in Ōita.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is also sometimes referred to as {{nihongo|'''Yusubara Hachiman-gū'''|由原八幡宮||}}. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''[[Hachiman]]'''|八幡||}}, here identified as the trinity of [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Empress Jingū]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend, which dates from the [[Kamakura period]] states that in the year 827, a monk of [[Enryaku-ji]] named Konki worshipped at [[Usa Hachiman-gū]] for 1000 days and received an oracle which stated that the ''kami'' Hachiman would bless Bungo Province. A mysterious white banner flew over Kakugo, [[Ōita District, Ōita| Ōita]] and was presented to [[Emperor Ninmyo]], who ordered the governor of Bungo Province to construct this shrine. From the year 998 onwards, it became the practice to reconstruct the shrine buildings at 33 year intervals, the same as at Usa Hachiman-gū. Despite its dispute with Sasumuta Jinja, which as an equally ancient claim to the title, it has been referred to as the "Bungo ''ichinomiya'' since the 12th century, because of the shrine's proximity to the ''[[kokufu]]'' of Bungo Province. The shrine was supported by the [[Ōtomo clan]] from the time they entered the province as ''[[shugo]]'' during the Kamakura period. In the [[Sengoku period]], it was destroyed by [[Ōtomo Sōrin]] after his conversion to Christianity, but was rebuilt in the early [[Edo period]] and supported by the successive ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Funai Domain]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. In 1912, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} .{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Yusuhara hachimangu Big gate.JPG|Torii Gate Higurashimon.JPG|Nandaimon Yusuhara hachimangu Nishimon.JPG|Nishimon Yusuhara hachimangu Roumon.JPG|'Moshidono, Heiden, Rōmon Inside Roumon.JPG|Inside the shrine buildings ==Cultural properties== ===Natural Monuments=== * ''[[Camphora officinarum]]'', National Natural Monument.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2851|title=柞原八幡宮のクス|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} Claimed to be over 3000 years old. Mentioned by [[Ino Tadataka]] in his 1811 survey of Japan ===[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]=== *'''Yusuhara Hachiman-gu''' (10 structures), Edo Period.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00004531|title=柞原八幡宮|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} ** Honden, 1850 ** Mōshidono, 1752 ** Haiden, 1759 ** Rōmon, 1760 ** Tōhōden, 1757 ** Saihōden, 1757 ** East Corridor, 1798 ** West Corridor, 1798 ** West Gate, late Edo Period ** South Great Gate, 1870, with storehouse including 4 documents: blueprints for Main Shrine and Rōmon, 2 maps of precincts) * '''[[Tachi]] sword''', Kamakura period, inscribed "Kunimune"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6405|title=太刀〈銘国宗／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Tachi sword''', Kamakura period, inscribed "Minamoto no Kuni?"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6404|title=太刀〈銘源国（以下一字竝年号不明）／〉〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''[[Halberd]]''', Nanboku-chō period, inscribed "Kunishige" and "Hachiman Daibodhisattva Tenma Daijizai Tenjin"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6406|title=薙刀直シ刀〈銘国重八幡大菩薩天満大自在天神／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Bronze Buddha statue''', [[Asuka period]]. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4648 |title=銅造仏像（社伝阿弥陀如來立像）|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Armor''', complete set, Muromachi period, {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/7221 |title=白檀塗浅葱糸威腹巻〈兜・大袖・小具足付／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} * '''Yusuhara Hachiman-gu old documents''', Heian period, 17 scrolls with 216 documents. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/10379 |title=柞原八幡宮文書（二百十六通）|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Yusuhara-hachimangu}} *[https://oita-yusuhara.com Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Bungo Province]] [[Category:Ōita (city)]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia25]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Yusuhara Hachimangū]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia25]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] [[en:Yusuhara Hachimangū]][[en:Yusuhara Hachimangū]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōita, Ōita, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yusuhara Hachiman-gū
柞原八幡宮 | image = YusuharaHachimangu Shaden.JPG | caption = ''Shaden'' of Yusuhara Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|14|18.2|N|131|33|3.6|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = c.830 | date_destroyed = | location = 987 Yawata, Ōita-shi, Ōita-ken | website = {{Official|1=https://oita-yusuhara.com}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = March 15 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yusuhara Hachiman-gū'''|柞原八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōita, Ōita | Ōita]], [[Ōita Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bungo Province]], the other being the [[Sasamuta Shrine|Sasamuta Jinja]], also in Ōita.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is also sometimes referred to as {{nihongo|'''Yusubara Hachiman-gū'''|由原八幡宮||}}. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''[[Hachiman]]'''|八幡||}}, here identified as the trinity of [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Empress Jingū]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend, which dates from the [[Kamakura period]] states that in the year 827, a monk of [[Enryaku-ji]] named Konki worshipped at [[Usa Hachiman-gū]] for 1000 days and received an oracle which stated that the ''kami'' Hachiman would bless Bungo Province. A mysterious white banner flew over Kakugo, [[Ōita District, Ōita| Ōita]] and was presented to [[Emperor Ninmyo]], who ordered the governor of Bungo Province to construct this shrine. From the year 998 onwards, it became the practice to reconstruct the shrine buildings at 33 year intervals, the same as at Usa Hachiman-gū. Despite its dispute with Sasumuta Jinja, which as an equally ancient claim to the title, it has been referred to as the "Bungo ''ichinomiya'' since the 12th century, because of the shrine's proximity to the ''[[kokufu]]'' of Bungo Province. The shrine was supported by the [[Ōtomo clan]] from the time they entered the province as ''[[shugo]]'' during the Kamakura period. In the [[Sengoku period]], it was destroyed by [[Ōtomo Sōrin]] after his conversion to Christianity, but was rebuilt in the early [[Edo period]] and supported by the successive ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Funai Domain]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. In 1912, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} .{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Yusuhara hachimangu Big gate.JPG|Torii Gate Higurashimon.JPG|Nandaimon Yusuhara hachimangu Nishimon.JPG|Nishimon Yusuhara hachimangu Roumon.JPG|'Moshidono, Heiden, Rōmon Inside Roumon.JPG|Inside the shrine buildings ==Cultural properties== ===Natural Monuments=== * ''[[Camphora officinarum]]'', National Natural Monument.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2851|title=柞原八幡宮のクス|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} Claimed to be over 3000 years old. Mentioned by [[Ino Tadataka]] in his 1811 survey of Japan ===[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]=== *'''Yusuhara Hachiman-gu''' (10 structures), Edo Period.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00004531|title=柞原八幡宮|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} ** Honden, 1850 ** Mōshidono, 1752 ** Haiden, 1759 ** Rōmon, 1760 ** Tōhōden, 1757 ** Saihōden, 1757 ** East Corridor, 1798 ** West Corridor, 1798 ** West Gate, late Edo Period ** South Great Gate, 1870, with storehouse including 4 documents: blueprints for Main Shrine and Rōmon, 2 maps of precincts) * '''[[Tachi]] sword''', Kamakura period, inscribed "Kunimune"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6405|title=太刀〈銘国宗／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Tachi sword''', Kamakura period, inscribed "Minamoto no Kuni?"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6404|title=太刀〈銘源国（以下一字竝年号不明）／〉〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''[[Halberd]]''', Nanboku-chō period, inscribed "Kunishige" and "Hachiman Daibodhisattva Tenma Daijizai Tenjin"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6406|title=薙刀直シ刀〈銘国重八幡大菩薩天満大自在天神／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Bronze Buddha statue''', [[Asuka period]]. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4648 |title=銅造仏像（社伝阿弥陀如來立像）|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Armor''', complete set, Muromachi period, {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/7221 |title=白檀塗浅葱糸威腹巻〈兜・大袖・小具足付／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} * '''Yusuhara Hachiman-gu old documents''', Heian period, 17 scrolls with 216 documents. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/10379 |title=柞原八幡宮文書（二百十六通）|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Yusuhara-hachimangu}} *[https://oita-yusuhara.com Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Bungo Province]] [[Category:Ōita (city)]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]] [[en:Yusuhara Hachimangū]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]] [[en:Yusuhara Hachimangū]][[en:Yusuhara Hachimangū]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōita, Ōita, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yusuhara Hachiman-gū
柞原八幡宮 | image = YusuharaHachimangu Shaden.JPG | caption = ''Shaden'' of Yusuhara Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|14|18.2|N|131|33|3.6|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = c.830 | date_destroyed = | location = 987 Yawata, Ōita-shi, Ōita-ken | website = {{Official|1=https://oita-yusuhara.com}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = March 15 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yusuhara Hachiman-gū'''|柞原八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōita, Ōita | Ōita]], [[Ōita Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bungo Province]], the other being the [[Sasamuta Shrine|Sasamuta Jinja]], also in Ōita.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is also sometimes referred to as {{nihongo|'''Yusubara Hachiman-gū'''|由原八幡宮||}}. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''[[Hachiman]]'''|八幡||}}, here identified as the trinity of [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Empress Jingū]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend, which dates from the [[Kamakura period]] states that in the year 827, a monk of [[Enryaku-ji]] named Konki worshipped at [[Usa Hachiman-gū]] for 1000 days and received an oracle which stated that the ''kami'' Hachiman would bless Bungo Province. A mysterious white banner flew over Kakugo, [[Ōita District, Ōita| Ōita]] and was presented to [[Emperor Ninmyo]], who ordered the governor of Bungo Province to construct this shrine. From the year 998 onwards, it became the practice to reconstruct the shrine buildings at 33 year intervals, the same as at Usa Hachiman-gū. Despite its dispute with Sasumuta Jinja, which as an equally ancient claim to the title, it has been referred to as the "Bungo ''ichinomiya'' since the 12th century, because of the shrine's proximity to the ''[[kokufu]]'' of Bungo Province. The shrine was supported by the [[Ōtomo clan]] from the time they entered the province as ''[[shugo]]'' during the Kamakura period. In the [[Sengoku period]], it was destroyed by [[Ōtomo Sōrin]] after his conversion to Christianity, but was rebuilt in the early [[Edo period]] and supported by the successive ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Funai Domain]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. In 1912, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} .{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Yusuhara hachimangu Big gate.JPG|Torii Gate Higurashimon.JPG|Nandaimon Yusuhara hachimangu Nishimon.JPG|Nishimon Yusuhara hachimangu Roumon.JPG|'Moshidono, Heiden, Rōmon Inside Roumon.JPG|Inside the shrine buildings ==Cultural properties== ===Natural Monuments=== * ''[[Camphora officinarum]]'', National Natural Monument.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2851|title=柞原八幡宮のクス|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} Claimed to be over 3000 years old. Mentioned by [[Ino Tadataka]] in his 1811 survey of Japan ===[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]=== *'''Yusuhara Hachiman-gu''' (10 structures), Edo Period.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00004531|title=柞原八幡宮|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} ** Honden, 1850 ** Mōshidono, 1752 ** Haiden, 1759 ** Rōmon, 1760 ** Tōhōden, 1757 ** Saihōden, 1757 ** East Corridor, 1798 ** West Corridor, 1798 ** West Gate, late Edo Period ** South Great Gate, 1870, with storehouse including 4 documents: blueprints for Main Shrine and Rōmon, 2 maps of precincts) * '''[[Tachi]] sword''', Kamakura period, inscribed "Kunimune"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6405|title=太刀〈銘国宗／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Tachi sword''', Kamakura period, inscribed "Minamoto no Kuni?"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6404|title=太刀〈銘源国（以下一字竝年号不明）／〉〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''[[Halberd]]''', Nanboku-chō period, inscribed "Kunishige" and "Hachiman Daibodhisattva Tenma Daijizai Tenjin"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6406|title=薙刀直シ刀〈銘国重八幡大菩薩天満大自在天神／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Bronze Buddha statue''', [[Asuka period]]. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4648 |title=銅造仏像（社伝阿弥陀如來立像）|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Armor''', complete set, Muromachi period, {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/7221 |title=白檀塗浅葱糸威腹巻〈兜・大袖・小具足付／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} * '''Yusuhara Hachiman-gu old documents''', Heian period, 17 scrolls with 216 documents. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/10379 |title=柞原八幡宮文書（二百十六通）|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Yusuhara-hachimangu}} *[https://oita-yusuhara.com Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} [[category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[category:Ichinomiya]] [[category:Bungo Province]] [[category:Ōita (city)]] [[category:Hachiman shrines]] [[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:kk]] [[en:Yusuhara Hachimangū]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:kk]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:kk]] [[en:Yusuhara Hachimangū]][[en:Yusuhara Hachimangū]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōita, Ōita, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yusuhara Hachiman-gū
柞原八幡宮 | image = YusuharaHachimangu Shaden.JPG | caption = ''Shaden'' of Yusuhara Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|14|18.2|N|131|33|3.6|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = c.830 | date_destroyed = | location = 987 Yawata, Ōita-shi, Ōita-ken | website = {{Official|1=https://oita-yusuhara.com}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = March 15 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yusuhara Hachiman-gū'''|柞原八幡宮||}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōita, Ōita | Ōita]], [[Ōita Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bungo Province]], the other being the [[Sasamuta Shrine|Sasamuta Jinja]], also in Ōita.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is also sometimes referred to as {{nihongo|'''Yusubara Hachiman-gū'''|由原八幡宮||}}. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main ''[[kami]]'' at this shrine is {{nihongo|'''[[Hachiman]]'''|八幡||}}, here identified as the trinity of [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Empress Jingū]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The foundation date of this shrine is unknown. The shrine's legend, which dates from the [[Kamakura period]] states that in the year 827, a monk of [[Enryaku-ji]] named Konki worshipped at [[Usa Hachiman-gū]] for 1000 days and received an oracle which stated that the ''kami'' Hachiman would bless Bungo Province. A mysterious white banner flew over Kakugo, [[Ōita District, Ōita| Ōita]] and was presented to [[Emperor Ninmyo]], who ordered the governor of Bungo Province to construct this shrine. From the year 998 onwards, it became the practice to reconstruct the shrine buildings at 33 year intervals, the same as at Usa Hachiman-gū. Despite its dispute with Sasumuta Jinja, which as an equally ancient claim to the title, it has been referred to as the "Bungo ''ichinomiya'' since the 12th century, because of the shrine's proximity to the ''[[kokufu]]'' of Bungo Province. The shrine was supported by the [[Ōtomo clan]] from the time they entered the province as ''[[shugo]]'' during the Kamakura period. In the [[Sengoku period]], it was destroyed by [[Ōtomo Sōrin]] after his conversion to Christianity, but was rebuilt in the early [[Edo period]] and supported by the successive ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Funai Domain]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was given the rank Prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. In 1912, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} .{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Yusuhara hachimangu Big gate.JPG|Torii Gate Higurashimon.JPG|Nandaimon Yusuhara hachimangu Nishimon.JPG|Nishimon Yusuhara hachimangu Roumon.JPG|'Moshidono, Heiden, Rōmon Inside Roumon.JPG|Inside the shrine buildings ==Cultural properties== ===Natural Monuments=== * ''[[Camphora officinarum]]'', National Natural Monument.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2851|title=柞原八幡宮のクス|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} Claimed to be over 3000 years old. Mentioned by [[Ino Tadataka]] in his 1811 survey of Japan ===[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]=== *'''Yusuhara Hachiman-gu''' (10 structures), Edo Period.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/00004531|title=柞原八幡宮|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} ** Honden, 1850 ** Mōshidono, 1752 ** Haiden, 1759 ** Rōmon, 1760 ** Tōhōden, 1757 ** Saihōden, 1757 ** East Corridor, 1798 ** West Corridor, 1798 ** West Gate, late Edo Period ** South Great Gate, 1870, with storehouse including 4 documents: blueprints for Main Shrine and Rōmon, 2 maps of precincts) * '''[[Tachi]] sword''', Kamakura period, inscribed "Kunimune"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6405|title=太刀〈銘国宗／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Tachi sword''', Kamakura period, inscribed "Minamoto no Kuni?"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6404|title=太刀〈銘源国（以下一字竝年号不明）／〉〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''[[Halberd]]''', Nanboku-chō period, inscribed "Kunishige" and "Hachiman Daibodhisattva Tenma Daijizai Tenjin"{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6406|title=薙刀直シ刀〈銘国重八幡大菩薩天満大自在天神／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Bronze Buddha statue''', [[Asuka period]]. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4648 |title=銅造仏像（社伝阿弥陀如來立像）|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} *'''Armor''', complete set, Muromachi period, {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/7221 |title=白檀塗浅葱糸威腹巻〈兜・大袖・小具足付／〉|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} * '''Yusuhara Hachiman-gu old documents''', Heian period, 17 scrolls with 216 documents. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/10379 |title=柞原八幡宮文書（二百十六通）|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External
